questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Seismograph in the Basement
Most visitors to the Ridge County Museum walked past the basement door without noticing the small sign that read “Seismic Station.” Behind the door, a sensitive instrument sat on a concrete pier that reached below the floor. The seismograph measured tiny movements in the ground, including vibrations from local construction, passing trucks, and distant earthquakes.
The museum had installed the station with help from a university geology department. At first, staff worried that the instrument would be useful only to scientists. Then a moderate earthquake occurred hundreds of miles away. No buildings in Ridge County were damaged, but the seismograph recorded the waves as they moved through the region. Museum educators printed the graph and displayed it upstairs with a simple explanation.
Visitors were surprised that an event so far away could leave a pattern in their town. The graph showed several wave types arriving at different times. A geologist explained that earthquake waves travel at different speeds, and those differences help scientists estimate where an earthquake began.
The station also helped separate facts from rumors. After a loud boom one evening, several residents posted online that an earthquake had occurred. The seismograph showed only a brief vibration pattern consistent with a nearby blast, not the longer wave pattern of an earthquake. The museum shared the explanation the next day.
The basement station made invisible motion visible. It reminded visitors that scientific evidence is often not dramatic at first glance, but it can answer questions that ordinary senses cannot.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A museum seismograph helped people understand ground movement and interpret events using evidence.
choices.B: Ridge County’s seismograph was useful only for recording passing trucks.
choices.C: Museum staff removed the seismograph because visitors disliked science displays.
choices.D: The seismograph proved that every loud sound is caused by an earthquake.

questions.correctAnswerText: A museum seismograph helped people understand ground movement and interpret events using evidence.
questions.explanation: The passage shows how the seismograph recorded distant earthquakes and helped explain a local boom using evidence.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seismograph in the Basement.”
Why did museum educators display the graph after the distant earthquake?

choices.A: To show visitors how a faraway earthquake could be recorded locally
choices.B: To prove that Ridge County buildings were badly damaged
choices.C: To advertise a construction project near the museum
choices.D: To replace all other museum exhibits with geology equipment

questions.correctAnswerText: To show visitors how a faraway earthquake could be recorded locally
questions.explanation: Educators displayed the graph so visitors could understand that distant seismic waves can be recorded in Ridge County.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Seismograph in the Basement.”
Which TWO events or activities could the seismograph record?

choices.A: Distant earthquakes
choices.B: Passing trucks
choices.C: Visitor opinions about exhibits
choices.D: Museum ticket sales

questions.correctAnswerText: Distant earthquakes | Passing trucks
questions.explanation: The passage says the seismograph could record distant earthquakes, passing trucks, construction, and other vibrations.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seismograph in the Basement.”
What can be inferred from the loud boom example?

choices.A: Seismograph data can help distinguish between different causes of ground vibration.
choices.B: Residents were correct that every boom must be an earthquake.
choices.C: The museum stopped sharing information after the boom.
choices.D: A nearby blast creates exactly the same pattern as a distant earthquake.

questions.correctAnswerText: Seismograph data can help distinguish between different causes of ground vibration.
questions.explanation: The instrument showed that the boom was consistent with a nearby blast rather than an earthquake.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seismograph in the Basement.”
In the sentence “The basement station made invisible motion visible,” invisible most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=not easily seen
questions.explanation: In context, invisible means that ground motion is not easy to detect with ordinary senses.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seismograph in the Basement.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: The seismograph recorded waves from an earthquake hundreds of miles away.
tiles.B: Different wave types arrived at different times.
tiles.C: The boom produced a brief vibration pattern consistent with a nearby blast.
tiles.D: The museum used the station to count daily visitors.

targets.T1: Evidence that distant events can be detected locally
targets.T2: Evidence that the graph contained information scientists could interpret
targets.T3: Evidence that the instrument helped correct a mistaken explanation

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=The seismograph recorded waves from an earthquake hundreds of miles away.; T2=Different wave types arrived at different times.; T3=The boom produced a brief vibration pattern consistent with a nearby blast.
questions.explanation: Each tile matches the requested evidence from the passage.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Confidential Shredding Procedure
To: Payroll, Human Resources, and Client Services Staff
From: Records Compliance Manager
Subject: Confidential Shredding Procedure
Beginning July 1, documents containing private information must be placed in locked shredding bins instead of regular trash or recycling containers. Private information includes Social Security numbers, bank account numbers, medical details, payroll records, client contracts, personnel files, and signed authorization forms.
Before placing a document in a shredding bin, employees should remove binder clips, plastic folders, and thick envelopes. Staples and small paper clips may remain. Do not leave confidential documents on top of the bin if the slot is full. If a bin is full or damaged, contact the records compliance manager immediately.
Drafts, duplicate copies, and outdated forms must be shredded if they contain private information. A document does not become safe for regular recycling simply because it is old or no longer needed. Employees working from home must return confidential paper records to the office for approved disposal unless a manager has authorized another secure process.
The shredding vendor empties the locked bins every Thursday and provides a certificate of destruction. This procedure protects clients, employees, and the company by reducing the risk that private information will be lost or exposed.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how staff should dispose of documents containing private information
choices.B: To tell staff to place payroll records in regular recycling bins
choices.C: To allow employees to leave confidential papers on top of full bins
choices.D: To announce that all records should be kept forever

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how staff should dispose of documents containing private information
questions.explanation: The document gives rules for using locked shredding bins and protecting confidential information.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Confidential Shredding Procedure.”
What should an employee do if a shredding bin is full?

choices.A: Contact the records compliance manager immediately.
choices.B: Leave documents on top of the bin.
choices.C: Put confidential documents in regular trash.
choices.D: Take the bin home until Thursday.

questions.correctAnswerText: Contact the records compliance manager immediately.
questions.explanation: Employees are told to contact the records compliance manager if a bin is full or damaged.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Confidential Shredding Procedure.”
Which TWO types of information are considered private in the document?

choices.A: Social Security numbers
choices.B: Bank account numbers
choices.C: Public holiday dates
choices.D: Office paint colors

questions.correctAnswerText: Social Security numbers | Bank account numbers
questions.explanation: Both are listed as examples of private information.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Confidential Shredding Procedure.”
Why must outdated forms be shredded if they contain private information?

choices.A: Private information still needs protection even if the document is old.
choices.B: Old documents always contain no useful information.
choices.C: Outdated forms must be mailed to every client.
choices.D: Recycling containers are emptied only once a year.

questions.correctAnswerText: Private information still needs protection even if the document is old.
questions.explanation: The document states that outdated forms with private information must still be shredded.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Confidential Shredding Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning July 1, documents containing private information must be placed in locked shredding bins.”
choices.B: “Staples and small paper clips may remain.”
choices.C: “The shredding vendor empties the locked bins every Thursday.”
choices.D: “This procedure protects clients, employees, and the company by reducing the risk that private information will be lost or exposed.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “This procedure protects clients, employees, and the company by reducing the risk that private information will be lost or exposed.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the purpose of the shredding procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Offer Optional Payroll Split for Emergency Savings
Employers should offer workers the option to split a small part of each paycheck into an emergency savings account. Many people want to save, but they wait until the end of the month and find that ordinary expenses have already used the money. Automatic saving at payday can make the process easier because the money is set aside before it is spent.
The program should be voluntary. Workers should choose whether to participate, how much to save, and when to change or stop the split. Even a small amount, such as $10 or $20 per paycheck, can build a cushion over time. That cushion may help a worker handle a car repair, medical copay, or temporary reduction in hours without turning immediately to high-cost borrowing.
Employers would benefit as well. Financial stress can distract workers and increase missed shifts when small emergencies become crises. A simple payroll split does not require the employer to give financial advice or manage the worker’s bank account. It only gives employees an easy way to direct part of their own pay.
Some critics may say that low wages, not saving habits, are the real problem. Higher wages matter, but that does not make savings tools useless. A payroll split will not solve every financial challenge, yet it can help workers who want a practical way to start building emergency savings.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Employers should offer optional payroll split because it can help workers build emergency savings gradually.
choices.B: Employers should require every worker to save the same amount.
choices.C: Workers should depend only on high-cost borrowing for emergencies.
choices.D: Payroll split programs should replace wages.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employers should offer optional payroll split because it can help workers build emergency savings gradually.
questions.explanation: The author argues that automatic payday saving can help workers build a cushion over time.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Optional Payroll Split for Emergency Savings.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that small automatic savings can become useful?

choices.A: Even $10 or $20 per paycheck can build a cushion over time.
choices.B: Employers should not manage workers’ bank accounts.
choices.C: Higher wages matter.
choices.D: Workers may choose to stop the split.

questions.correctAnswerText: Even $10 or $20 per paycheck can build a cushion over time.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports the claim that small automatic savings can become useful.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Offer Optional Payroll Split for Emergency Savings.”
Which TWO features does the author say workers should control?

choices.A: How much to save
choices.B: When to change or stop the split
choices.C: The employer’s tax rate
choices.D: The bank accounts of coworkers

questions.correctAnswerText: How much to save | When to change or stop the split
questions.explanation: The author says workers should control participation, savings amount, and changes to the split.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Optional Payroll Split for Emergency Savings.”
How does the author respond to the criticism that low wages are the real problem?

choices.A: By acknowledging higher wages matter while arguing that savings tools can still help
choices.B: By claiming low wages never affect workers
choices.C: By saying workers should be forced to save more
choices.D: By arguing that financial stress is imaginary

questions.correctAnswerText: By acknowledging higher wages matter while arguing that savings tools can still help
questions.explanation: The author recognizes the wage concern but explains that savings tools can still provide practical support.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Optional Payroll Split for Emergency Savings.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 2: [D1]

D1: A workplace survey found that employees who used automatic savings were more likely to have money available for small emergencies after six months.
The payroll office has two desks and a locked supply cabinet.
Some employees prefer to receive pay on Fridays.

questions.correct: D1=A workplace survey found that employees who used automatic savings were more likely to have money available for small emergencies after six months.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A workplace survey found that employees who used automatic savings were more likely to have money available for small emergencies after six months.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the claim that automatic savings can help workers prepare for emergencies.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Optional Payroll Split for Emergency Savings.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Some workers are more likely to save when money is set aside automatically at payday.
choices.B: Every worker has enough savings for emergencies already.
choices.C: Employers should decide how employees spend all wages.
choices.D: Emergency expenses never affect work attendance.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some workers are more likely to save when money is set aside automatically at payday.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that automatic saving makes saving easier for some workers.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Window Display
Priya had worked at the bookstore café for eight months, but she had never arranged the front window. That job belonged to Aaron, who could stack novels, mugs, and paper stars so that customers stopped on the sidewalk. On Monday, Aaron called in sick, and the manager pointed to three boxes near the counter.
“Local authors week starts tomorrow,” the manager said. “Use these.”
Priya opened the boxes. Inside were novels, memoirs, poetry collections, and a children’s book about a girl who built a kite from newspaper. She wanted the window to look full, but when she placed every book in a straight line, the display looked like a shelf that had been moved forward by mistake.
She started again. This time she chose five covers with strong colors and placed them at different heights. She leaned the kite book against a blue mug and used folded paper to lift a poetry collection near the center. A customer waiting for coffee watched silently, then said, “That red cover is hiding the title behind it.”
Priya felt her face warm, but the customer was right. She shifted the book. The display opened up, as if the items had finally taken a breath.
The next morning, a woman came in and asked for “the kite book in the window.” Priya walked to the children’s section with her. On the way back, she glanced at the display. It did not look like Aaron’s work. That was why she liked it.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Priya must create a bookstore window display without relying on someone else’s usual style.
choices.B: Priya tries to convince the manager to cancel local authors week.
choices.C: Priya argues with Aaron about where to store café mugs.
choices.D: Priya refuses to arrange any books in the window.

questions.correctAnswerText: Priya must create a bookstore window display without relying on someone else’s usual style.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Priya’s attempt to make the display work in her own way.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Window Display.”
Why does Priya start over after placing every book in a straight line?

choices.A: The display looks flat and unappealing, like a shelf moved forward.
choices.B: The manager tells her that local authors week has ended.
choices.C: All the books are damaged and cannot be shown.
choices.D: The customer asks her to remove every item from the window.

questions.correctAnswerText: The display looks flat and unappealing, like a shelf moved forward.
questions.explanation: Priya recognizes that the straight-line arrangement does not create an effective display.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Window Display.”
What does Priya’s response to the customer’s comment show?

choices.A: She is willing to use helpful feedback to improve the display.
choices.B: She refuses to change the display once someone comments on it.
choices.C: She believes customers should not look at the window.
choices.D: She decides that titles do not matter in a bookstore display.

questions.correctAnswerText: She is willing to use helpful feedback to improve the display.
questions.explanation: Priya accepts the customer’s observation and adjusts the red cover.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Window Display.”
Which TWO details show Priya making the display more visually effective?

choices.A: She places books at different heights.
choices.B: She shifts the red cover so it no longer hides another title.
choices.C: She puts every book in a straight line.
choices.D: She closes all three boxes without using them.
choices.E: She waits for Aaron to return before doing any work.

questions.correctAnswerText: She places books at different heights. | She shifts the red cover so it no longer hides another title.
questions.explanation: Both actions improve how the display looks and communicates.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Window Display.”
What does the final sentence suggest about Priya?

choices.A: She gains confidence in creating something that reflects her own judgment.
choices.B: She decides that Aaron’s displays were always poor.
choices.C: She believes the display failed because it attracted a customer.
choices.D: She plans to avoid all future creative tasks.

questions.correctAnswerText: She gains confidence in creating something that reflects her own judgment.
questions.explanation: Priya likes the display because it is not simply an imitation of Aaron’s work.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Window Display.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Priya’s first arrangement looks like a shelf moved forward.
tiles.B: The customer points out that one cover hides another title.
tiles.C: A woman asks for “the kite book in the window.”
tiles.D: Priya throws away the local authors’ books.

targets.T1: Evidence that Priya’s first attempt does not work well
targets.T2: Evidence that feedback helps Priya notice a problem
targets.T3: Evidence that the display attracts customer interest

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Priya’s first arrangement looks like a shelf moved forward.; T2=The customer points out that one cover hides another title.; T3=A woman asks for “the kite book in the window.”
questions.explanation: The tiles show the weak first attempt, useful feedback, and customer interest.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding a Balance Transfer Offer
A balance transfer offer allows a credit card customer to move debt from one account to another, often with a low introductory interest rate for a limited time. At first, the offer may look like a simple way to save money. However, customers should read the terms carefully before deciding whether the transfer is useful.
One important detail is the balance transfer fee. A card may charge 3 percent or 5 percent of the amount transferred. For example, a 5 percent fee on a $2,000 transfer would add $100 to the balance. A low interest rate may still save money, but the fee should be included in the comparison.
Another key detail is the length of the introductory period. If the balance is not paid before that period ends, a higher interest rate may apply to the remaining amount. Customers should estimate whether they can make enough monthly payments to reduce the balance before the regular rate begins.
A balance transfer also does not solve overspending by itself. If a person moves debt to a new card but continues adding charges to the old card, the total debt may grow. The offer can be useful when it is part of a repayment plan. Without a plan, it may only move the problem from one account to another.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A balance transfer offer can help some customers, but fees, timing, and repayment habits must be considered.
choices.B: A balance transfer always eliminates debt immediately.
choices.C: Customers should accept every low-interest offer without reading the terms.
choices.D: Balance transfer fees apply only when no money is transferred.

questions.correctAnswerText: A balance transfer offer can help some customers, but fees, timing, and repayment habits must be considered.
questions.explanation: The passage explains possible benefits while warning about fees, introductory periods, and repayment behavior.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Balance Transfer Offer.”
Why should customers consider the balance transfer fee?

choices.A: The fee adds to the amount owed and affects whether the transfer saves money.
choices.B: The fee always cancels the introductory interest rate.
choices.C: The fee is paid only by the old credit card company.
choices.D: The fee proves that the transfer is always a bad choice.

questions.correctAnswerText: The fee adds to the amount owed and affects whether the transfer saves money.
questions.explanation: The balance transfer fee becomes part of the cost and must be included in the comparison.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Balance Transfer Offer.”
What may happen if the balance is not paid before the introductory period ends?

choices.A: A higher interest rate may apply to the remaining amount.
choices.B: The debt automatically disappears.
choices.C: The transfer fee is always refunded.
choices.D: The old card pays the balance for the customer.

questions.correctAnswerText: A higher interest rate may apply to the remaining amount.
questions.explanation: The passage says a regular higher rate may apply after the introductory period ends.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Balance Transfer Offer.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that a balance transfer should be part of a broader repayment plan?

choices.A: “A balance transfer offer allows a credit card customer to move debt from one account to another.”
choices.B: “A card may charge 3 percent or 5 percent of the amount transferred.”
choices.C: “If a person moves debt to a new card but continues adding charges to the old card, the total debt may grow.”
choices.D: “One important detail is the balance transfer fee.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “If a person moves debt to a new card but continues adding charges to the old card, the total debt may grow.”
questions.explanation: This sentence shows why a transfer needs to be part of a repayment plan.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Balance Transfer Offer.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It explains what a balance transfer is, identifies key terms, and warns about repayment behavior.
choices.B: It tells a story about one customer losing a credit card.
choices.C: It lists every credit card company in order of size.
choices.D: It argues that customers should never compare interest rates.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains what a balance transfer is, identifies key terms, and warns about repayment behavior.
questions.explanation: The passage defines the offer, discusses fees and timing, and warns about continued spending.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Overtime Approval Reminder
(1) Employees who expect to work overtime must receive supervisor approval before the extra hours begin. (2) The request should include the project name, reason for overtime, estimated hours, and deadline. (3) Supervisors use this information to decide whether the work should continue after the regular shift. (4) [D1], employees should not assume that staying late automatically qualifies for overtime pay. (5) The approval form [D2] submitted to payroll by noon on Monday. (6) Employees should keep a copy of the approved form for their records.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore; D2=must be
questions.explanation: “Therefore” connects approval information to the warning about staying late, and “must be submitted” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Overtime Approval Reminder.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Employees should keep a copy of the approved form for their records.
choices.B: Employees should keep, for the purpose of their records, a copy of the form that has approval.
choices.C: A copy of the approved form should be kept by employees for records they have.
choices.D: Employees, if they want records, should make sure to keep a copy of the form that is approved.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employees should keep a copy of the approved form for their records.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and preserves the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The forms near the payroll folder needs signatures.
choices.B: The list of overtime requests are on the supervisor’s desk.
choices.C: The employees who work late record their hours carefully.
choices.D: The packet of time sheets have been copied.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employees who work late record their hours carefully.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “employees” correctly takes the plural verb “record.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Taylor called Morgan after the shift, she updated the form.
choices.B: Taylor called Morgan after the shift, and Taylor updated the form.
choices.C: She updated it when Taylor called Morgan after the shift.
choices.D: After the shift, she called her and updated it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Taylor called Morgan after the shift, and Taylor updated the form.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Taylor” makes clear who updated the form.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The deadline moved from Friday to Wednesday. [D1], the supervisor approved two hours of overtime.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore
questions.explanation: “Therefore” shows that the overtime approval resulted from the moved deadline.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The form was complete: payroll processed the overtime request.
choices.B: The form was complete payroll processed the overtime request.
choices.C: The form was complete, payroll processed the overtime request.
choices.D: The form was complete: and payroll, processed the overtime request.

questions.correctAnswerText: The form was complete: payroll processed the overtime request.
questions.explanation: The colon correctly connects a complete sentence to a closely related result.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because the supervisor approved the request.
choices.B: After the employee finished the extra work.
choices.C: Payroll processed the approved overtime form.
choices.D: While reviewing the project deadline.

questions.correctAnswerText: Payroll processed the approved overtime form.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The supervisor reviewed the request and signs the form yesterday.
choices.B: The supervisor reviews the request and signed the form yesterday.
choices.C: The supervisor reviewed the request and signed the form yesterday.
choices.D: The supervisor had reviewed the request and signs the form yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor reviewed the request and signed the form yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The request asks for project name, estimated hours, and whether the deadline changed.
choices.B: The request asks for project name, estimated hours, and deadline changes.
choices.C: The request asks for naming the project, estimated hours, and to change the deadline.
choices.D: The request asks for project name, hours that are estimated, and if the deadline changed.

questions.correctAnswerText: The request asks for project name, estimated hours, and deadline changes.
questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Use Anonymous Employee Feedback Surveys
A company should use anonymous employee feedback surveys twice a year. Employees are often closest to daily problems, such as confusing procedures, uneven workloads, unclear communication, or equipment delays. However, some workers may hesitate to speak honestly if they believe criticism could affect their schedule, promotion chances, or relationship with a supervisor.
Anonymous surveys can reveal patterns that managers might otherwise miss. If many employees in one department report unclear instructions, the issue is probably larger than one person’s complaint. If several teams identify the same bottleneck, leaders can decide where to investigate first. Surveys should include both rating questions and space for specific examples.
The company should still encourage direct conversation. Anonymous surveys are not a replacement for good supervision or team meetings. They are one tool for collecting honest input, especially from employees who are less comfortable speaking publicly. If leaders share general results and action steps afterward, surveys can build trust rather than simply collect complaints.
Passage B: Use Open Feedback Discussions Instead
A company should not rely on anonymous employee surveys. While anonymity may encourage honesty, it can also produce vague comments that are difficult to address. A statement such as “communication is bad” does not show which process failed, who needs information, or what change would help. Managers may spend time guessing instead of solving the real issue.
Open feedback discussions create clearer accountability. In small group meetings, employees can explain concerns, answer follow-up questions, and suggest practical solutions. Supervisors can clarify misunderstandings immediately and identify which problems require higher-level action. A conversation can turn frustration into a plan more quickly than a survey report can.
Anonymous surveys may also increase mistrust if employees never see meaningful results. Workers may wonder who reads the responses or whether comments are ignored. The company should focus on training managers to invite feedback respectfully and respond without defensiveness. Honest workplace communication requires relationships, not just forms.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports anonymous surveys as one way to collect honest input, while Passage B argues open discussions lead to clearer solutions.
choices.B: Passage A argues that managers should ignore employees, while Passage B argues that workers should never give feedback.
choices.C: Passage A supports eliminating team meetings, while Passage B supports banning conversations.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on credit cards, while Passage B focuses on bookstore displays.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports anonymous surveys as one way to collect honest input, while Passage B argues open discussions lead to clearer solutions.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about whether anonymous surveys or open discussions are better for employee feedback.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about employee feedback.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: Employees may hesitate to speak honestly if criticism could affect work relationships or opportunities.
choices.B: Vague comments can be difficult to address.
choices.C: Small group meetings allow follow-up questions.
choices.D: Managers can clarify misunderstandings immediately.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employees may hesitate to speak honestly if criticism could affect work relationships or opportunities.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s argument for anonymous feedback.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about employee feedback.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Anonymous comments may be too vague for managers to solve.
choices.B: Open discussions allow employees to answer follow-up questions and suggest solutions.
choices.C: Employees should never criticize company procedures.
choices.D: Anonymous surveys always solve every workplace problem.
choices.E: Managers should avoid hearing employee concerns.

questions.correctAnswerText: Anonymous comments may be too vague for managers to solve. | Open discussions allow employees to answer follow-up questions and suggest solutions.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both points as reasons to prefer open discussion.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about employee feedback.
Which statement from Passage A directly acknowledges a limitation of anonymous surveys?

choices.A: “A company should use anonymous employee feedback surveys twice a year.”
choices.B: “Anonymous surveys can reveal patterns that managers might otherwise miss.”
choices.C: “The company should still encourage direct conversation.”
choices.D: “Surveys should include both rating questions and space for specific examples.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The company should still encourage direct conversation.”
questions.explanation: This sentence acknowledges that anonymous surveys should not replace direct communication.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about employee feedback.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors want employees’ concerns to be heard, but they disagree about the best method.
choices.B: Both authors believe employee feedback has no value.
choices.C: Both authors argue that supervisors should never respond to concerns.
choices.D: Both authors think anonymous surveys should replace all management decisions.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors want employees’ concerns to be heard, but they disagree about the best method.
questions.explanation: Both passages value feedback, but they recommend different ways to collect it.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about employee feedback.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data comparing response rates, issue clarity, employee trust, and completed improvements after surveys and open discussions
choices.B: A list of office wall colors
choices.C: A history of the company’s parking rules
choices.D: A survey asking employees which snacks they prefer

questions.correctAnswerText: Data comparing response rates, issue clarity, employee trust, and completed improvements after surveys and open discussions
questions.explanation: These data would help evaluate the effectiveness of both feedback methods.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Filed in the payroll folder, Dana reviewed the overtime forms before noon.

choices.A: Filed in the payroll folder, Dana reviewed the overtime forms before noon.
choices.B: Dana reviewed the overtime forms filed in the payroll folder before noon.
choices.C: Dana, filed in the payroll folder, reviewed the overtime forms before noon.
choices.D: The payroll folder reviewed Dana’s overtime forms before noon.

questions.correctAnswerText: Dana reviewed the overtime forms filed in the payroll folder before noon.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “overtime forms,” the items filed in the folder.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The supervisor approved a request. The request listed two overtime hours.

choices.A: The supervisor approved a request that listed two overtime hours.
choices.B: The request approved the supervisor and listed two overtime hours.
choices.C: Listing the supervisor, two overtime hours approved a request.
choices.D: The supervisor approved, and two overtime hours listed the request.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor approved a request that listed two overtime hours.
questions.explanation: This sentence combines the ideas clearly without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The supervisor’s signature appeared at the bottom of the form.
choices.B: The supervisors signature appeared at the bottom of the form.
choices.C: The supervisors’ signature’s appeared at the bottom of the form.
choices.D: The supervisor signatures’ appeared at the bottom of the form.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor’s signature appeared at the bottom of the form.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the signature belongs to one supervisor.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Use Automatic Service Charges Instead of Traditional Tips
Restaurants should replace traditional tipping with automatic service charges that are clearly listed on menus and receipts. Under the traditional tipping system, servers’ income can depend on customer habits, weather, table assignments, and biases that have little to do with the quality of service. A clear service charge would create more predictable pay and reduce the pressure on customers to calculate a tip at the end of the meal.
An automatic charge can also support the whole staff. A restaurant meal depends not only on the server but also on hosts, bussers, dishwashers, cooks, and food runners. A service charge can be distributed according to a written policy so that more of the team shares in the income created by good service. Customers would know the cost before ordering, which makes the bill less surprising.
Restaurants would need to be transparent. Menus should state the charge clearly, receipts should show where it appears, and managers should explain how the money supports employees. If the charge is hidden or confusing, it would create the same frustration as unclear fees in other industries.
Traditional tipping is familiar, but familiarity does not make it fair. A clear automatic service charge can make restaurant pay more stable while still allowing customers to compliment excellent service directly if they choose.
Passage B:
Keep Traditional Tipping
Restaurants should keep traditional tipping rather than replacing it with automatic service charges. Tipping gives customers a direct way to reward excellent service. A server who is attentive, knowledgeable, and efficient can earn more because customers choose to recognize that effort. Removing the customer’s choice may make the dining experience feel less personal.
Automatic service charges can also confuse customers. Some diners may not understand whether the charge goes to the server, the whole staff, or the restaurant. Others may add a tip on top of the charge because they feel unsure, making the meal more expensive than expected. Even when the charge is listed on the menu, customers may overlook it until the bill arrives.
Restaurants also vary widely. A small diner, a fine-dining restaurant, and a counter-service café may not need the same system. Traditional tipping allows flexibility across different business models. If a restaurant wants to use a service charge, it can already choose to do so, but the industry should not move away from tipping entirely.
The better approach is transparency within the current system: clear tip suggestions, honest job postings about pay, and better training so customers know when a service charge is already included.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether restaurants should replace traditional tipping with automatic service charges? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
questions.correctAnswerText: Not auto-scored
questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
